items.passage: Arctic terns undertake the longest yearly migration of any bird, traveling between the Arctic and Antarctic regions. Each autumn these small seabirds leave their northern breeding grounds and follow winding routes across entire oceans. They chase the endless summer sunlight, feeding on fish and tiny creatures near the surface. Strong winds and ocean currents are used cleverly to save precious energy along the way. By the time they return, some birds have flown a distance equal to several trips around the planet.

items.passage: Mangrove forests grow along tropical coastlines where salty seawater and muddy land meet in a challenging environment. Their tangled roots rise above the water and hold the loose soil firmly in place. These sturdy roots also shelter young fish and countless crabs from larger hunting animals. During violent storms the dense trees absorb powerful waves and protect the villages behind them. Because they store large amounts of carbon, these coastal forests help slow the warming of the global climate.

items.passage: Spaced repetition is a study method in which learners review information at gradually increasing time intervals. Instead of cramming everything at once, students revisit each fact several days apart. This spacing forces the brain to work harder, which strengthens the memory each time. Research shows that material learned this way is remembered far longer than material studied quickly. Many popular language learning applications are now built around this simple but powerful principle.

items.passage: The printing press was one of the most influential inventions in the long history of human communication. Before its arrival, books were copied slowly by hand and remained rare and expensive. The new machine pressed inked metal letters onto paper, producing many identical pages quickly. Suddenly ideas could spread across whole countries within weeks rather than many years. This flood of cheap books helped ordinary people learn to read and think for themselves.

items.passage: Soil is a living mixture of tiny mineral grains, decayed plants, water, air, and microscopic creatures. It forms very slowly as weather and roots break solid rock into fine particles. Fallen leaves rot on the surface and add rich nutrients that feed new growth. Different layers, called horizons, can be seen clearly in the walls of a deep pit. Healthy soil supports nearly all the crops that people and animals depend on for food.

items.passage: The surface of the Moon is covered with thousands of round craters of many different sizes. Most of these craters were formed when rocky objects from space crashed into the ground. Because the Moon has almost no air, these ancient scars are never worn away. Some craters are surrounded by bright streaks of dust thrown out by the impact. By studying their shapes, scientists can learn how old different parts of the Moon are.

items.passage: Many small bats are able to hunt in complete darkness by using sound rather than sight. They send out rapid high-pitched squeaks that bounce off nearby insects and walls. The returning echoes tell the bat exactly where its tiny prey is flying. This clever system, known as echolocation, works well even on the darkest nights. Using it, a single bat can catch hundreds of mosquitoes in one busy hour.

items.passage: Plants cannot walk, so they have developed many clever ways to spread their seeds across the land. Some seeds grow tiny wings or soft hairs that let the wind carry them. Others hide inside sweet fruit that is eaten and later dropped by hungry birds. A few clever seeds have small hooks that cling tightly to the fur of animals. In these different ways, young plants can begin life well away from their crowded parents.

Others hi__ inside sw___ fruit th__ is eaten and later dropped by hungry birds. A few clever seeds have small hooks that cling tightly to the fur of animals. In these different ways, young plants can begin life well away from their crowded parents.

items.passage: Fermentation is a natural chemical process that living microbes use to turn sugars into other substances. Tiny yeasts and bacteria feed on sugar and release gas, acid, or alcohol as waste. People have used this quiet process for thousands of years to make bread and cheese. The bubbles produced by yeast are what cause fresh dough to rise in the oven. Without these helpful microbes, many familiar foods and drinks would simply not exist.

items.passage: Sound is a form of energy that travels through the air as invisible waves of pressure. When an object shakes quickly, it pushes the air molecules around it back and forth. These moving molecules bump into their neighbors and pass the energy along like a chain. The waves finally reach the ear, where they are changed into nerve signals for the brain. Sound moves faster through water and solid metal than it does through ordinary air.

items.passage: Thunderstorms develop on warm, humid days when the lower air near the ground heats up quickly. The heated air rises fast and forms tall, dark clouds full of water droplets. Inside these clouds, strong currents lift and drop the droplets until they collide. This constant rubbing builds up electricity that is released as a bright flash of lightning. The sudden heat from the flash makes the air explode outward, creating loud thunder.

items.passage: Writing was invented independently by several ancient peoples who needed a lasting way to record important information. The earliest marks were simple pictures that stood for goods, animals, or amounts of grain. Over many centuries these pictures slowly became the abstract symbols now known as letters. Written records allowed rulers to collect taxes and keep track of distant events. Thanks to writing, the thoughts of people long dead can still be read today.

items.passage: Coral reefs are colorful underwater structures that are built slowly by millions of tiny sea animals. Each little animal, called a polyp, surrounds itself with a hard cup of stone. As old polyps die, new ones grow on top, and the reef slowly rises. These crowded reefs provide food and shelter for a quarter of all ocean species. Warm, clean, sunlit water is needed for the delicate corals to stay alive and healthy.

items.passage: Green plants make their own food through photosynthesis, a process powered by ordinary light from the sun. Their leaves absorb sunlight and pull in a gas called carbon dioxide from the air. Using water drawn up from the roots, they build sugar that stores this captured energy. As a useful byproduct, the leaves release the oxygen that most living things must breathe. In this quiet way, plants form the base of nearly every food chain on Earth.

items.passage: Many islands in the middle of the ocean were created long ago by erupting underwater volcanoes. Hot melted rock rises from deep below and slowly piles up on the sea floor. After countless eruptions, the growing mountain finally breaks above the surface of the waves. Wind and birds then bring seeds, and green plants begin to cover the bare rock. Over time these lonely islands can develop unusual animals found nowhere else on the planet.

items.passage: Comets are small icy objects that travel around the sun along very long and narrow oval paths. For most of their journey they stay frozen in the cold outer regions of space. As a comet comes closer to the sun, its ice heats up and turns to gas. This escaping gas and dust form a glowing tail that always points away from the sun. Some famous comets return to the night sky only once every several human lifetimes.

items.passage: The Silk Road was a vast network of trade routes that once linked the East and West. Merchants led long trains of camels loaded with silk, spices, and precious stones. The dangerous journey crossed high mountains, dry deserts, and lands ruled by many kings. Along with valuable goods, travelers also carried new ideas, religions, and useful inventions. In this way, very distant cultures came to shape one another thousands of miles apart.

items.passage: The magnetic compass is a simple instrument that always shows travelers which direction is true north. Its heart is a thin magnetic needle that can spin freely on a fine point. The needle lines up with the natural magnetic field that surrounds the whole planet. Long ago, sailors used this device to cross open seas far from any land. Before the compass, ships often stayed close to the coast to avoid getting lost.

items.passage: Pottery is one of the oldest crafts, made by shaping soft clay and hardening it with heat. Early people dug clay from riverbanks and pressed it into simple bowls and pots. When the wet clay was baked in a hot fire, it became solid and waterproof. These sturdy containers let families store grain, carry water, and cook their daily meals. Broken pieces of ancient pottery still teach us how people lived in the distant past.

items.passage: The bystander effect describes how people are less likely to help a stranger when others are near. In a large crowd, each person quietly assumes that someone else will step forward. Because the responsibility feels widely shared, no single individual actually decides to take action. Researchers first noticed this surprising pattern while studying reactions to public emergencies. Simply knowing about the effect can encourage a person to help despite the silent crowd.
